item.title: Albert Hirschman Strikes Back
item.description: The globalization to which we have grown accustomed and prospered from has survived recessions, financial crises, and a worldwide pandemic, but it may not survive today's escalating geoeconomic conflict. A book published more than 80 years ago – and all but ignored at the time – explains why.

item.title: Epstein and the Globalization of Criminal Exploitation
item.description: While the Jeffrey Epstein affair continues to receive global attention, the true scale of human trafficking and modern slavery remains underappreciated, and the perpetrators almost always go unpunished. G20 countries can start cracking down on this scourge immediately by focusing on six priorities.

item.title: India’s Promising New Counter-Terrorism Strategy
item.description: India's new counter-terrorism strategy seeks to replace the reactive and disjointed approach of recent decades with a holistic doctrine that supports unity and coordination across India’s vast and often fragmented security apparatus. But the path from an eight-page document to a safer reality is littered with practical hurdles.

item.title: A Deal With Iran Requires an Iran that Can Make One
item.description: US President Donald Trump’s Venezuela gambit worked, to the extent that it did, because the state did not collapse. This might not be the outcome in Iran, because the Trump administration's war partners, Israel and the Kurds, have their own interests and ambitions, which do not necessarily include avoiding the fragmentation of authority.

item.title: China’s Big Bet on Central Asia is Paying Off
item.description: The Western caricature of Chinese “debt-trap diplomacy” ignores the realities of China’s economic presence in Central Asia. What is emerging across the region is not dependency but interdependence, as Chinese foreign direct investment finances industrialization and mutually beneficial infrastructure projects.

item.title: Europe’s “Limited Responsibility” Model Must Go
item.description: In interventions at an informal European Council meeting and the Munich Security Conference, European Central Bank President Christine Lagarde recently explained exactly what Europe must do to secure its prosperity and sovereignty in the years ahead. The question now is whether European leaders will heed the call.

item.title: An AI Bubble Won’t Trigger a Financial Crisis
item.description: The AI boom may be speculative, excessive, and reminiscent of earlier episodes like the dot-com crash. But given the nature of the financing and the investments being made, the risks to the financial system are minimal, and policymakers should turn their attention to the impact of AI on the real economy.

item.title: Can the US Afford Trump’s Iran War?
item.description: With the outlook for US employment and inflation already becoming increasingly grim, President Donald Trump's war of choice against Iran is bound to weaken public finances and the broader economy further, creating yet another reason for international investors to think twice about buying US debt. What ever happened to America First?

item.title: Hungary and the Future of the EU
item.description: The European Union’s central challenge is to defend its members against external aggression, whether from the US or Russia, and Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orbán’s regime has long frustrated this effort. Even if Orbán is defeated in this year's election, EU leaders must take the right lessons from his 16 years of illiberal rule.

item.title: The Economic Magic of Equal Opportunities for Women
item.description: None of the 190 countries covered by the World Bank’s Women, Business, and the Law 2026 report provides women with the same legal environment as men, with the biggest gaps found in safety, entrepreneurship, and childcare. In developing economies, the costs in terms of growth and employment are considerable.

item.title: Kevin Warsh Is in for a Rude Awakening
item.description: For years, Kevin Warsh, Donald Trump’s nominee to serve as the next chair of the US Federal Reserve, has been staking out policy positions that would almost certainly backfire if put into practice. Fortunately, market conditions and the rest of the central bank's board will still have a say in monetary policymaking.

item.title: What Turkey Wants in Iran
item.description: While avoiding protracted instability in Iran is vital to Turkey’s interests, so is ensuring that the Islamic Republic does not emerge victorious from the current war. Turkey's ideal scenario – a managed degradation of Iran’s ambitions and capabilities – might be best served by a Venezuela-style leadership transition.

item.title: A Stronger Work Ethic Won’t Fix Advanced Economies
item.description: German Chancellor Friedrich Merz learned the wrong lesson on his recent trip to China. Advanced economies expand and remain competitive not through additional labor inputs but through capital deepening, technological progress, and total factor productivity growth.

item.title: Financial Insecurity, Not Immigration, Is Driving Populist Politics
item.description: Nearly a decade after Brexit and Donald Trump’s first election victory, populism is still often portrayed as a revolt by working-class voters struggling to keep up with economic change. But today’s electoral shifts reflect everyday forms of insecurity affecting a much broader segment of the population.

item.title: Reclaiming Democracy From the Market
item.description: Meritocracy promised a fairer society, but instead it has deepened the divide between winners and losers and eroded the dignity of work. As AI reorders economies and concentrates power in fewer and fewer hands, the question is no longer whether democracy needs saving, but whether we can save it in time.

item.title: Iran’s Energy-Warfare Strategy
item.description: In its response to Operation Epic Fury, Iran appears to be exploiting the structural fragility of the global energy trade, since even a temporary closure of the Strait of Hormuz could have far-reaching global consequences. If energy infrastructure continues to be targeted as well, the risk will extend beyond prices.

item.title: Breaking Up Iran Would Be Catastrophic
item.description: It may suit Israel to foment separatist movements in Iran, but that doesn’t mean it serves the interests of the United States or its NATO allies. Western powers have a very poor track record with such strategies, and that would not change with a Balkanized Iran, which would produce one crisis after another.

item.title: The Iran War Is Upending Global Energy Markets
item.description: The longer the US-Israeli campaign against Iran lasts, the greater the implications for the global energy system. Whereas short conflicts produce volatility, prolonged instability reshapes everything from trade flows to assessments of infrastructure risk and long-term investment behavior.

item.title: What Makes America Strong?
item.description: In 2025, US net migration was likely negative, as the number of legal immigrants has been reduced in various ways. The overall effect will be to slow economic growth – perhaps by a lot – relative to what it otherwise could be.

item.title: The Birth of European Nuclear Deterrence
item.description: With French President Emmanuel Macron's announcement of a new doctrine of "forward deterrence," Europe is finally building a nuclear strategy that fully belongs to Europeans. The age of strategic dependence on the United States is drawing to a close.
